coredata.dc:description: In this work, to meet the requirement of nitrite detection, a ratiometric colorimetric sensor was constructed for sensitive and rapid detection of nitrite using Ce–Mn@CoOOH nanozyme. The Ce–Mn@CoOOH nanozyme was prepared by the hydrothermal method, and exhibited excellent oxidase-like activity. Under the catalysis of Ce–Mn@CoOOH nanozyme, colorless TMB can be oxidized to blue TMB complex. Furthermore, the blue TMB complex was further oxidized and diazotized by nitrite, and then respectively produce yellow oxTMB (diimine derivative) and diazonium salt under acidic conditions. There was a good linear correlation between the absorbance (A652/A452) and nitrite concentrations in the range of 0.3–35 μg mL−1 with a low detection of 0.017 μg mL−1. In addition, the developed method was verified using ion chromatography. Importantly, a smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensor was designed for visual detection of nitrite, enabling convenient, low-cost and on-site analysis of nitrite in food.

head.ce:title: Ce–Mn@CoOOH nanozyme for constructing a ratiometric colorimetric sensor for rapid and sensitive detection of nitrite in food

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, to meet the requirement of nitrite detection, a ratiometric colorimetric sensor was constructed for sensitive and rapid detection of nitrite using Ce–Mn@CoOOH nanozyme. The Ce–Mn@CoOOH nanozyme was prepared by the hydrothermal method, and exhibited excellent oxidase-like activity. Under the catalysis of Ce–Mn@CoOOH nanozyme, colorless TMB can be oxidized to blue TMB complex. Furthermore, the blue TMB complex was further oxidized and diazotized by nitrite, and then respectively produce yellow oxTMB (diimine derivative) and diazonium salt under acidic conditions. There was a good linear correlation between the absorbance (A652/A452) and nitrite concentrations in the range of 0.3–35 μg mL−1 with a low detection of 0.017 μg mL−1. In addition, the developed method was verified using ion chromatography. Importantly, a smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensor was designed for visual detection of nitrite, enabling convenient, low-cost and on-site analysis of nitrite in food.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite is a common food additive, used in preservatives, color protection, antibacterial agents and flavor preservation. Therefore, it is favored by the food industry (Altunay and Elik, 2020; Karwowska and Kononiuk, 2020). However, nitrite is a potentially toxic substance, and excessive nitrite will threaten the human health. Nitrite can combine with hemoglobin to form methemoglobin leading to fatal hypoxia in infants, or react with amino acids to form nitrosamines, inducing gastric and esophageal cancer (Shpaizer et al., 2018; Zarringhalami, Sahari, and Hamidi-Esfehani, 2009). Furthermore, nitrite as a common water pollutant can pose a potential threat to the ecological environment (Zeng et al., 2024). Therefore, it is necessary to use nitrite in a regular, reasonable and legal manner. The Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives (JECFA) has also ruled that the maximum nitrite intake should be 0.06 mg/kg/day (Wu et al., 2018). Based on the national safety standard of China GB 2760–2014, the maximum amount of nitrite in meat enema meat products is 0.5 g/kg, and the maximum residue is 30 mg/kg. Therefore, achieving accurate, rapid and sensitive detection of nitrites is crucial for both food quality safety control and ecological environment protection.

Till date, numerous methods have been developed for nitrite detection, such as HPLC (He et al., 2011; Lin et al., 2019), ultraviolet spectrophotometry (Lin et al., 2011), electrochemical methods (Wu et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2018), capillary electrophoresis (Farsang et al., 2022), fluorescence methods (Fu et al., 2019) and colorimetric methods (Amanulla et al., 2017; Taweekarn et al., 2022). These measurement methods have the advantages of high accuracy, good reliability and high precision. These traditional methods mainly rely on expensive, large, complex instruments and specialized operators. Recently, colorimetric methods are widely used in physical and chemical analyses because of their considerable advantages, such as fast response, simplicity, obvious phenomena, visualization, and high sensitivity (Chen et al., 2020). Therefore, colorimetric methods have received considerable attention for the quantitative analysis and detection of nitrite (Sepahvand, Ghasemi, and Seyed Hosseini, 2021). Meanwhile, most of colorimetric methods are based on a single signal change generated by a chemical reaction involving nitrite, which has limited detection capability for lower concentration targets and is highly susceptible to interference by external factors during the actual detection process (Choodum et al., 2020; Hao et al., 2023; Hao et al., 2022; Man et al., 2024). To improve the detection accuracy, ratiometric colorimetric sensors have gradually become the main research direction. Generally, the ratiometric absorbance value is used as the analytical signal to achieve quantitative detection of target substances. This approach can effectively avoid errors caused by external factors such as detection environment, reagent concentration, and improper operation, thereby improving the reliability, reproducibility, and resistance to interference in the final detection results (Wang et al., 2022). Therefore, it's necessary to develop a ratiometric colorimetric sensor that can achieve accurate detection of nitrite.

Enzyme-mimetic nanomaterials, also known as nanozymes, are a new generation of artificial enzymes possessing both the unique properties of nanomaterials and catalytic functionality (Ding et al., 2023). These nanozymes cover a wide range of natural enzyme catalytic activities, such as peroxidase, oxidase, and glucose oxidase activities, and are widely used in various fields such as food, medical diagnosis, environmental and materials science (Wei et al., 2022). Compared to other enzyme-free methods, nanozymes possessed their unique advantages, specific can be summarized in several aspects. (1) The catalytic character of nanozymes could offer amplified signals for high-sensitivity analysis; (2) Nanozymes could be employed as catalytic labels and cooperate with biological recognition elements (natural enzymes, aptamers, antibodies/antigens, and polypeptides) for target analysis; (3) Nanozyme-based analytical methods could be combined with pattern recognition and artificial intelligence for high-throughput screening and smart diagnosis (Tian et al., 2024). As is well known, natural enzymes are extremely fragile, and a slight change in the external environment may cause a decrease in catalytic capacity, or even loss of activity (Du et al., 2023). Moreover, the purification of natural enzymes involves high-cost and time-consuming procedures, which limit their related analytical applications. Compared to natural enzymes, nanozymes offer advantages such as low cost, excellent stability, good tolerance, and the potential for large-scale mass production, which supports industrial development (Qiao et al., 2024).

In recent years, metal-based nanozymes have attracted extensive attention in the fields of analytical chemistry, antisepsis, anti-inflammatory, cancer treatment and so on. Among them, the mutual synergistic effect between different metal valences makes multimetallic nanozymes possess better catalytic ability and physicochemical properties than single metal nanozymes, which undoubtedly has attracted the attention and enthusiasm of researchers (Luo et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2017). High electron transfer rates between the metal active centers on the surface of metal-based nanozymes are beneficial for enhancing the electron transfer rate and can provide a sufficient number of binding sites for reaction participation. And, more active sites mean better catalytic ability (Lee et al., 2024; Zhu et al., 2024). However, single enzyme activity, an unclear internal catalytic mechanism, and a lack of specificity pose serious challenges for future research on metal-based nanozymes (Liu et al., 2021).

Inspired by the above investigations, we successfully synthesized a Ce–Mn@CoOOH nanozyme with superior oxidase-like activity. The preparation process is shown in Scheme 1A. And then, the corresponding catalytic mechanism of Ce–Mn@CoOOH nanozyme was revealed. Then, a ratiometric colorimetric sensor was designed for the rapid and sensitive detection of nitrite based on the Ce–Mn@CoOOH nanozyme. In addition, to achieve sensitive, visual, and on-site detection of nitrite, we developed a smartphone-assisted portable detection platform, as shown in Scheme 1B. Importantly, the final test results confirmed that the ratiometric colorimetric sensor could be successfully applied in actual food samples.

The reagents and instruments used in the experiments are described in the supplementary material. The synthesis of Ce–Mn@CoOOH referred to the Peng group’s report with some modifications (Peng et al., 2023). First, 0.0287 g of Mn (NO3)2·6 H2O and 0.0434 g of Ce (NO3)3·6 H2O were transferred into a 50 mL beaker containing 25 mL of deionized water, and the mixture was sonicated. Then, 0.0158 g of potassium permanganate was dissolved in a 100 mL beaker containing 25 mL of deionized water. The metal salt ion mixture solution was added dropwise to potassium permanganate solution at room temperature, stirred continuously for 30 min, and then reacted at 110 °C for 10 h. Finally, the precipitate obtained was washed with anhydrous ethanol and deionized water. In the second step, the product obtained from the first step was dispersed into a 50 mL beaker containing 25 mL of deionized water. Then, 0.029 g of Co(NO3)2·6 H2O was added and sonicated for 10 min. Subsequently, 0.0079 g of KMnO4 was placed in a 100 mL beaker containing 25 mL of deionized water. The remaining operations were the same as in the first step. The obtained precipitate was dried in an oven at 60 °C and named Ce–Mn@CoOOH. Finally, Ce–Mn@CoOOH was milled into powder and stored tightly sealed at room temperature for subsequent use.

The Exploration of the oxidase-like activity of Ce–Mn@CoOOH is described in the supplementary material. The feasibility of the developed method was verified by recording the characteristic response signals of the detection system with different concentrations of nitrite. First, 16 μL of Ce–Mn@CoOOH suspension (1 mg/mL), 20 μL of TMB solution (20 mM), and 100 μL of acetate buffer solution (pH 3.0, 0.2 M) were mixed and incubated for 5 min. Afterward, 50 μL of the above reaction solution was mixed with 450 μL of the solution containing different concentrations of nitrite and incubated for 20 min at room temperature. Then, 200 μL of the solution was placed in a 96-well plate. Finally, the UV-Vis spectrum in the range of 390–800 nm was measured using a multimode microplate reader. Besides, to evaluate the specific recognition ability of the colorimetric detection platform, some common cations (Na+, Ga2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, K+), anions (F−, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, HCO3 −, Cl−, SO4 2−), glucose, and other common substances were selected as interference sources.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nitrite detection in actual food samples
ce:section.ce:para: Actual food samples used for testing included sausages, pickles, and tap water. Sausages and pickles were subjected to additional pretreatment. The food sample processing method was based on a previous study (Zhang et al., 2023). First, 5.0 g of sausages or pickles was placed to a 50 mL centrifuge tube. Then, 29 mL of deionized water was added. The mixture was sonicated for 1 h and centrifuged at 9500 rpm for 20 min. Then, the supernatant was retained. The other procedure was the same for sausages, pickles, and tap water. Freshly prepared NaF (1 mL, 1 mM) solution and milled activated carbon powder (300 mg) were added to each of the extracted supernatant and 29 mL of tap water. NaF can effectively remove Fe3+ interference from samples, and activated carbon powder can adsorb colored substances, metal ions, and organic compounds. The solution was allowed to stand for 5 h and then centrifuged at 9500 rpm for 15 min. Finally, the supernatant was filtered through an organic phase filtration facility. The filtrate was successively passed through the H and the RP column to obtain the final detection liquid samples, which were stored at 4 ℃ and promptly arranged for subsequent testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of a smartphone-assisted nitrite detection sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve on-site and visual detection, an intelligent and portable platform was further developed based on color changes with the addition of different concentrations of nitrite solution. The reaction solutions were then added to cuvettes of the same size and placed in natural light to allow the smartphone to take a picture. The self-developed color recognition software “Color Reader” was used to evaluate the color of the selected area of the photo and convert it into RGB values, and finally establish the mathematical relationship between the RGB value and NO2 − concentration. In the actual sample detection, the accurate concentration of NO2 − can be obtained by taking pictures and analyzing according to the above operating procedure.

The microscopic morphology of Ce–Mn@CoOOH was obtained from SEM images, as displayed in Fig. 1 A and B. The surface of Ce–Mn@CoOOH exhibited numerous flaky folds. Owing to the accumulation of Ce–Mn@CoOOH, the prepared material exhibited an irregular and dense network-like pore structure. According to the particle size distribution map (Fig. S1A), the average particle size of Ce–Mn@CoOOH was around 447.6 nm. In addition, to analyze the surface area and pore size distribution, the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) and Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) tests were carried out, and the test results are shown in Fig. S1B. The specific surface area of Ce–Mn@CoOOH was 62.00 m2/g and the average pore size was 3.63 nm. Besides, Ce–Mn@CoOOH exhibited a standard type IV adsorption isotherm with H-3 type hysteresis loop in the pressure range of P/P0 = 0.4–1.0, indicating that Ce-Mn@CoOOH was an irregular mesoporous material formed by nonrigid aggregation (Liu et al., 2015). The test results were consistent with the SEM images. In general, a larger specific surface area can expose more binding and reactive sites, which is conducive to the contact between TMB and Ce–Mn@CoOOH and promotes the catalytic reaction. Conversely, Fig. 1C-E show the TEM images of Ce–Mn@CoOOH at different magnifications. Ce–Mn@CoOOH exhibited some larger cores and pronounced lamellar structure, which was attributed to the formation of Ce–Mn-based metal oxides and the surface covering CoOOH flakes. Meanwhile, the element mapping images (Fig. 1 F) also clearly showed that Ce–Mn@CoOOH contained four elements, Ce, Mn, Co, and O, all of which were evenly distributed on the surface.

To identify the elemental information and material composition of Ce–Mn@CoOOH, XPS analysis was conducted, as shown in Fig. S2 and Fig. 2A–2D. Fig. 2A shows a the detailed photoelectron spectrum of asymmetric Ce 3d. The peaks at 886.5 and 905.5 eV were generated by Ce3+ and the other peaks at 883.5, 889.9, 898.8, 901.9, 908.7, and 917.3 eV were ascribed to Ce4+, indicating that Ce in Ce–Mn@CoOOH mainly exists in the form of CeO2 (Zhang et al., 2022). Ce3+ has been confirmed to have a strong relationship with oxygen vacancies because Ce3+ may increase the number of oxygen vacancies and improve the migration efficiency of oxygen. Therefore, the presence of Ce3+ conferred catalytic activity on Ce–Mn@CoOOH. The peaks at 654.2 and 642.6 eV in Fig. 2B were derived from the spin–orbit coupling of Mn 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2, respectively. The peaks at 641.8, 642.7, and 644.6 eV correspond to Mn2+, Mn3+, and Mn4+, respectively (Guo et al., 2024). The area occupied by Mn3+ was the largest, followed by Mn4+ and Mn2+.

According to the research reported by Li’s group, the introduction of Co promoted the shift of the Mn 2p characteristic peak to a higher binding energy position, indicating the phenomenon of Co doping into higher valence Mn (Li et al., 2021). The phenomenon suggested enhanced interactions between metal ions (Zhang et al., 2020). Both these aspects were key factors in promoting catalytic activity. As shown in Fig. 2C, the Co 2p photoelectron spectrum had eight distinct Gaussian peaks. The peaks at 781.0 and 796.0 eV belonged to Co3+ and those at 782.6 and 797.6 eV were ascribed to Co2+. The other peaks were their corresponding vibrational satellite peaks. The intensity of Co3+ was greater than that of Co2+, indicating the high activity of nanozymes (Luo et al., 2017). Finally, the present state of oxygen in Ce-Mn@CoOOH was studied by analyzing the photoelectron spectrum of O 1 s. As shown in Fig. 2D, Ce–Mn@CoOOH has three types of oxygen (Zhang et al., 2015), namely, lattice oxygen species (Olatt, 529.9 eV), surface adsorbed oxygen species (Oads, 531.6 eV), and hydroxyl groups (Oγ, 533.1 eV). The Oads/Oall value was 0.33, indicating a significant adsorbed oxygen ratio. A large amount of Oads is conducive to the recirculation of reactive oxygen species, which benefits the catalytic activity.

Ce–Mn@CoOOH was further explored by XRD to obtain detailed crystal structure information (Fig. 2E). The 2θ characteristic peaks at 20.23°, 38.88°, 50.58°, and 65.33° were attributed to the (003), (012), (015) and (110) planes of CoOOH (JCPDF#07–0169) (Fu et al., 2021). No significant diffraction peaks related to Ce or Mn were observed, which may be due to the existence of an amorphous structure in Ce–Mn@CoOOH (Yi et al., 2023). On the other hand, the surface-coated CoOOH may mask some of the diffraction peaks that may be present. As a result, XRD only observed the characteristic diffraction peaks of CoOOH.

In addition, Ce–Mn@CoOOH was analyzed by Raman spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2F, the characteristic peaks at 460 cm−1 were attributed to the overlapping vibrations of the Co–O and Ce–O bonds (Zhang et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2020). Meanwhile, the peaks at 564 and 640 cm−1 were attributed to the typical stretching vibrations of the Mn–O bond in MnO2 and of the Mn–O–Mn bond in Mn3O4, respectively (Kumar, Matoh, Kaur, and Štangar, 2021; Lao, Jiang, Huang, Zhang, and Wang, 2020). The results indicated that Mn existed in Ce-Mn@CoOOH in multiple oxidation states, consistent with the XPS results. These characterizations confirmed the successful synthesis of Ce–Mn@CoOOH.

As shown in Fig. 3 A, no characteristic peak appeared at 652 nm when Ce–Mn@CoOOH or TMB existed alone, which was consistent with the blank control group. Only when TMB was present with Ce–Mn@CoOOH, a strong absorbance appeared at 652 nm. In addition, the effects of Ce3+, Mn2+, and Co2+ on the TMB were investigated, as shown in Fig. S3. There was no change in absorbance at 652 nm, indicating that none of the TMB was oxidized. This suggested that TMB was oxidized due to the oxidase-like properties of Ce-Mn@CoOOH, rather than the possible interference of Ce3+, Mn2+ and Co2+. Fig. 3B shows the Michaelis–Menten kinetic curve obtained using different concentrations of TMB as catalytic substrates. Then, the Km and Vmax values of Ce–Mn@CoOOH catalytic TMB were calculated to be 0.0686 mM and 0.077 μM s−1 from the Lineweaver-Burk plot (Fig. 3 C). The low Km value indicated that Ce-Mn@CoOOH had excellent oxidase-like activity.

Numerous studies have showed that free dissolved oxygen in solution is essential for the oxidase-like catalytic activity of nanozymes (Luo et al., 2019). To confirm the effect of dissolved oxygen on the catalytic activity of Ce–Mn@CoOOH, oxygen removal experiments were performed. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the reaction system showed normal absorbance under oxygen-rich conditions, while the absorbance decreased when the reaction system was treated with nitrogen for 20 min, suggesting that the catalytic activity of Ce–Mn@CoOOH was inhibited. This result strongly proved that Ce–Mn@CoOOH was a catalyst rather than an oxidant, and the catalytic capacity of Ce–Mn@CoOOH was closely related to dissolved oxygen.

Besides, in order to further explore the types of free radicals generated by Ce–Mn@CoOOH, free radical scavenging experiments and EPR studies were carried out. Here, PBQ, L-tryptophan and IPA were selected as scavengers of the superoxide anion radical (O2 •−), singlet oxygen radical (1O2) and hydroxyl radical (•OH), respectively. As shown in Fig. 4B, the relative ratio of absorbance at 652 nm decreased significantly and decreased more obviously with increasing PBQ concentration, while the relative activities of the other experimental groups did not decrease significantly, which proved that plenty of O2 •− and very small amounts of 1O2 and •OH were produced during the catalytic process. In addition, the EPR spectra (Fig. 4 C) of different time periods showed that the longer the incubation time, the stronger the characteristic signal of 1:1:1:1 belonging to O2 •−. These results revealed that Ce-Mn@CoOOH could effectively catalyze the conversion of dissolved oxygen into O2 •− as an effective ROS species. Finally, O2 •− oxidized the colorless TMB to the blue TMB complex. At the same time, due to the introduction of Co, there was a dual-cycle oxidation pathway in Ce–Mn@CoOOH: Mn3+ + Ce4+ ↔ Mn4+ + Ce3+ and Mn3+ + Co3+ ↔ Mn4+ + Co2+. Therefore, it was conjectured that the following process occurred during the catalysis:
(1) Mn3+ + Ce4+ ↔ Mn4+ + Ce3+
(2) Ce3+ + O2 → Ce4+ + O2 •−
(3) Mn3+ + O2 → Mn4+ + O2 •−
(4) Co2+ + O2 → Co3+ + O2 •−
(5) Mn3+ + Co3+ ↔ Mn4+ + Co2+
(6) 8H++3TMB + 2O2 •−→3oxTMB+4H2O

To elucidate the mechanism of nitrite detection in the Ce–Mn@CoOOH–TMB system, we conducted a deeper investigation. Fig. S4 shows the SEM images of Ce–Mn@CoOOH before and after the addition of nitrite, and there was no significant difference observed between both. In addition, the FTIR spectrum (Fig. S5) displayed that the peaks belonging to nitrite appeared near 1170–1350 cm−1 and 820–850 cm−1 with the introduction of nitrite. However, the other peaks did not change in any way, indicating that nitrite did not cause any change in the surface group or structure of Ce–Mn@CoOOH. These results confirmed that nitrite did not interact with Ce–Mn@CoOOH, suggesting that the color change may be attributed to the reaction of nitrite with the TMB complexes under acidic conditions.

To verify the above conjecture, a series of experiments were designed. Fig. S6A shows the color change of the TMB complex solution and the peroxidized TMB mixed solution after the addition of the strong reducing agent ascorbic acid (AA), respectively. The final mixed solution became colorless, indicating that the TMB complex and oxTMB can be reduced. Moreover, the TMB complex solution turned yellow–green after adding nitrite for 20 min (Fig. S6B), at this time, TMB complex, oxTMB and diazonium salt all existed in the solution. Then, the above solution changed from yellow-green to pale yellow with the addition of AA. Because the TMB complex and oxTMB were reduced, only the nonreducible diazonium salt remained in the solution.

In summary, Ce–Mn@CoOOH caused TMB to lose one electron and thus be oxidized to the blue TMB complex, which exhibited its maximum absorption at 652 nm. However, nitrite can cause the TMB complex to lose an electron again and further oxidize the TMB complex to yellow oxTMB, which has the maximum absorption at 452 nm and can be reduced to colorless TMB (Zhang et al., 2020). Conversely, the primary amine groups in the TMB complex were highly susceptible to diazotization with nitrite under acidic conditions to form a yellow diazonium salt (Liu et al., 2023). The substance exhibited maximum absorption at 452 nm and was not reducible. Over time, the diazonium salt eventually decomposes into diazotized TMB in aqueous solution. The diagram of the detection mechanism based on Ce–Mn@CoOOH–TMB for nitrite detection can be summarized as Scheme S1.

Similar to natural enzymes, the pH, temperature, concentration, and incubation time greatly affect the catalytic ability of Ce–Mn@CoOOH, thus, these factors were optimized to ensure the best catalytic activity of Ce–Mn@CoOOH. Fig. S7A shows the relative activity of Ce–Mn@CoOOH at different pH conditions. As the pH increased, relative activity increased and reached a maximum at pH 3. Then, the relative activity decreased with the increase of pH. The catalytic activity was lower at pH 2 than that at pH 3, mainly because excess H+ caused TMB peroxidation (Yue et al., 2023). However, with increasing pH, H+ was not conducive to the Ce–Mn@CoOOH catalyzed TMB, resulting in a decrease of the absorbance at 652 nm. Hence, pH 3 was selected in the subsequent experiments. The relationship between the Ce–Mn@CoOOH concentration and relative activity is shown in Fig. S7B, indicating that the optimal state was achieved at a Ce–Mn@CoOOH concentration was 118 μg mL−1, so 118 μg mL−1 of Ce–Mn@CoOOH was used as the final experimental condition. In addition, the effect of temperature on the catalytic activity of Ce–Mn@CoOOH was investigated, and the test results are shown in Fig. S7C. In general, the catalytic activity of Ce–Mn@CoOOH was inhibited with the increase of temperature. While, even at the high temperature of 90 °C, Ce–Mn@CoOOH retained more than 60 % of its catalytic activity, indicating that Ce–Mn@CoOOH had good high temperature resistance compared with natural enzymes. Finally, to ensure detection accuracy, the incubation time was also optimized. As shown in Fig. S7D, the absorbance ratio (A652/A452) gradually decreased and remained stable after 20 min, indicating that the TMB complex fully reacted with nitrite at this time. Thus, 20 min was chosen as the incubation time for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of nitrite using the Ce–Mn@CoOOH-based detection platform
ce:section.ce:para: Photographs of the Ce–Mn@CoOOH–TMB system at different concentrations of nitrite are shown in Fig. 5 A. And the corresponding UV-Vis spectra are shown in Fig. 5B. As shown in Fig. 5B, the absorbance at 652 nm decreased gradually and the absorbance at 452 nm increased with increasing nitrite concentration from 0 to 45 μg mL−1. Therefore, a mathematical relationship between the A652/A452 and nitrite concentration was established (Fig. 5 C), and when the nitrite concentration reached 35 μg mL−1, A652/A452 remained stable. There was a good linear relationship between A652/A452 and the logarithm of the nitrite concentration (Fig. 5D). The linear equation was y = −1.629x + 2.612 (R2 = 0.997). The limit of detection was calculated to be 0.017 μg mL−1, which was far below the harmful concentration threshold of nitrite in food.

More importantly, compared with the previous methods for the rapid detection of nitrite ( Table 1), the method established in this work has a wider detection range or lower detection limit. It has shown unique advantages for the detection of nitrite in food and the guarantee of food safety.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance evaluation of the ratiometric colorimetric sensor platform
ce:section.ce:para: As a nanozyme, its anti-interference ability, stability and reproducibility are essential for practical use. Fig. S8 shows the sensitivity of the colorimetric sensor to common interfering substances. Except for Fe3+, the remaining interfering substances had no significant effect on the detection of NO2 −, indicating that the constructed ratiometric colorimetric sensor had good anti-interference ability. Fortunately, when Fe3+ coexists with a high concentration of F−, a colorless [FeF6]3− complex will be formed and significantly reduce the interference caused by Fe3+ (Zhang et al., 2023), providing a solution for detecting NO2 − in the presence of Fe3+.

Simultaneously, to determine the stability of Ce–Mn@CoOOH, the catalytic capacities of Ce–Mn@CoOOH at different storage periods were compared, and the comparison results are shown in Fig. S9A. After up to 30 days of storage, the catalytic activity of Ce–Mn@CoOOH was still within an acceptable range, suggesting its excellent stability. The longer storage cycle provides a solid basis for further commercial use of Ce–Mn@CoOOH. Fig. S9B shows a comparison of the catalytic activity of Ce–Mn@CoOOH synthesized in different batches, and the differences in catalytic activity among the five batches were not significant under the same catalytic conditions, confirming that the synthesis of Ce–Mn@CoOOH had good reproducibility.

To verify the practical applicability of the developed method, we measured the nitrite contents in pickles, sausages and tap water. The test results are displayed in Table 2. The good recovery (89.33 % –103.00 %) and low relative standard deviation (0.71 % – 4.14 %) indicated that the proposed method had good reproducibility and reliability for nitrite detection in real samples. Meanwhile, the detection results of ion chromatography were introduced as a control to ensure the reliability and accuracy of the experimental results. The above results illustrated that this proposed strategy had high accuracy and reliability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the smartphone-assisted nitrite detection sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: A smartphone-assisted colorimetric method was further developed based on color variation ( Fig. 6 A). Fig. 6B shows the RGB values of different solutions, and the B values decreased with the increase of NO2 − concentrations. Thus, a linear relationship between the B value and the logarithm of NO2 − concentration was established (Fig. 6 C) with a linear equation of y = −65.192x + 132.452 (R2 = 0.995) in the range of 2−35 μg mL−1 with an LOD of 0.93 μg mL−1. The calculation formula for LOD was LOD=3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation and k is the slope of the standard curve. The reliability and practicability of the smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing platform were verified by the standard addition method and the results were shown in Table S1. Compared to the standalone colorimetric sensor, the smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensor offered the advantages of low cost, portability, and ease of use, which can play a unique role in certain situations.

In addition, we compared the variation between the colorimetric method and the smartphone-assisted platform by adding standard concentrations of nitrite to the samples based on the results of the IC. The data in Table 2 and Table S2 have been analyzed using one-way ANOVA in SPSS software, and the differences between samples were evaluated by performing the T-test (p = 0.05). As shown in the Table S2, p<0.05 means that there is a significant difference between the two groups of data. Different lowercase letters represent significant (p<0.05) differences between groups in the same figure. Specifically, in Group “Detecting pickles”, if two data have the same letter, it indicates that there is no significant difference between the detection results of colorimetric method and those of smartphones when the same amount of nitrite is added. However, when different amounts of nitrite are added, each method has different letters for the detection results of nitrite, implying significant differences. This means that two methods are feasible for detecting nitrite in pickles. Same for Group “Detecting sausage.

As shown in Table S1, 5, 10, and 15 mg kg−1 nitrite were added to the samples of pickles and sausages, respectively. On the whole, there was no significant difference in the detection results by colorimetric method and smartphone assisted platform, except for the 15 mg kg−1 pickles group. Meanwhile, there was a significant difference in the detection results between the two methods when different concentrations of nitrite were added, which indicates that the methods can accurately analyze the differences between different concentrations. In addition, the results of intra assay recovery experiment (Table S3) indicate that the colorimetric method has a certain degree of accuracy and reliability. Therefore, this method strategy is feasible for detecting nitrite in real samples.

Herein, we developed a ratiometric colorimetric sensor based on Ce–Mn@CoOOH nanozyme and revealed the catalytic mechanism of Ce–Mn@CoOOH nanozyme. The ratiometric colorimetric sensor exhibited high sensitivity toward nitrite in the range of 0.3–35 μg mL−1 with a low detection limit of 0.017 μg mL−1. Importantly, the absorbance ratios at 652 nm and 452 nm were chosen as the characteristic signals of the nitrite concentration, effectively minimizing errors caused by external environmental factors and food matrices. Furthermore, the introduction of smartphone-assisted technology enabled the rapid on-site detection of nitrite. The proposed method provided an important solution for rapid detection of nitrite in remote and resource-limited areas. Thus, this study provides a new methodological approach for nitrite detection and offers theoretical and technical support for developing nanozyme-based analytical methods.
